item.title: Re: you can't smoke in here!
item.content:encoded: I agree. I wonder if he just exists, or if he is death itself.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Obligatory reminder that the SCP wiki is not a roleplay site, and posts are expected to be made out-of-character.

item.title: What happens I take SCP500 after being visited by this guy?
item.content:encoded: Will I not die?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A little fridge logic: maybe one of the reasons why the Foundation is not willing to in any way contain or fight with 4999 is the fact that many of them know that they have high chances of seeing him.

Look at the criteria: I'd guess a lot of agents are military veterans, and even if they are not prior to serving in the Foundation, the service is pretty much like military service. They have life consuming work that they cannot talk about which probably destroys family life (look at how cops and soldiers often divorce). And they have high chance of being stranded somewhere in some strange place dying on their own, maybe only accompanied by bodies of collegagues who died just before them.

If I had such work, I would also hope for a guy to come with a cigarette to come to me send me off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So you're telling me the only way to meet this guy is to have no money, have no friends, and have no family? Well shit, know I know what I'm spending the last few years of my life doing.

+1

item.title: Yay! Another friendly Keter!
item.content:encoded:
I love KETERs that aren't scary or evil, like the moon champion, or his brother. The main "danger" is the threat of exposure!

An instant +1 for me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, people? See how a Keter-class SCP doesn't need to be a world-ending doomsday device, a horrible monster or even anything remotely dangerous, and still work?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted because of that last sentence and the malinconic vibes.

item.title: Coolio
item.content:encoded: So when reading this i understand that it is hard to contain but sense it rarely manifests why is it a keter

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Such a nice scp and heart warming scp

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hello

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, its unconventional, and thats what makes it good, so great job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article, so delicately and gently, touches on what I think might be the most universal fear we all have, dying alone.

item.title: Deeply effecting
item.content:encoded: This is a little personal, but I had to watch my dad die recently and I thought about this one a lot. Someone To Watch Over Us is a lot more meaningful when you're actually watching someone die. He didn't need a mysterious cigarette smoking man because he had family, and my mother laying in bed holding him, but, oof, this is a bittersweet and comforting.

item.title: Best Series V story
item.content:encoded: if not best, my favorite of the bunch, absolutely

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP was really unique to me. it isn't a killer shadow or murderous invisible monsters. It's just a man who comfort those who has no one by them on their deathbed. +1

item.title: Re: Keter?
item.content:encoded:
4999 still does leave a trace (the cigarette) and can be quite easily detected through recordings. So while the Foundation is aware of the kinds of people 4999 targets, they would have to keep all of them under close surveillance to make sure information about it doesn't leak.

And I would note the use of the word "effectively". While 4999's effects aren't that disruptive, the Foundation still has no way to stop it or actually contain it. It keeps occurring and the Foundation can currently only cover its tracks and hope it doesn't become any more severe.

Also, if you actually want to push for a change in classification, you should contact the article's author CadaverCommander.

item.title: Re: Keter?
item.content:encoded:
I would like to challenge the keter classification. SCP-4999 is stated to be effectively self containing, due to its anomalous properties actually working to help ensure secrecy. The only possible way1 that it could be keter is if the foundation had trouble suppressing what little information a publically available source could get their hands on… but that's a bit of a stretch considering that the foundation does have access to AI who go through all available forms of media, seeking out even the slightest evidence of an anomaly.

So basically I think the ACS for SCP-4999 should look like this:

[[include :scp-wiki:component:anomaly-class-bar-source
|item-number= 4999
|clearance= 3
|container-class= euclid
|secondary-class= none
|disruption-class= vlam
|risk-class= notice
]]

Any questions?

Footnotes
1. At least in my mind.

item.title: Re: Keter?
item.content:encoded:
Looking at the actual box analogy from the Object Classes guide

If you lock it in a box, leave it alone, and it easily escapes, then it's probably Keter.

As you can see, the box test is about how easily the anomaly escapes, not about how dangerous it is. This goes for object classes as a whole, as stated further down the Object Classes guide

No, danger does not really affect an SCP's Object Class. As has been reiterated several times above this, an item's Object Class is more based on the difficulty of containment rather than the danger it otherwise poses. For example, a button that can destroy the entire universe when it's pressed would be safe, whereas a cat who randomly switches places with another cat anywhere on earth would be considered Keter.

The Foundation has no way to stop SCP-4999 from doing what it is doing, which makes it quite uncontained, i.e. Keter. The author has even decided to illustrate the situation through the extra classifications, with the Risk Class (representing danger) being 1 and the Disruption Class (representing how hard it is to conceal) being 4.

item.title: Keter?
item.content:encoded: I love this post and think it is a great scp, but I disagree with the Keter classification. I think Euclid is much more fitting. Going by the box analogy, scp 4999 is a non-endangering scp. I'm asking for it to be reclassified, but that doesn't stop me from giving it a +1.

item.title: really cool
item.content:encoded:
i really like this scp. definitely a good idea behind it, and was fun to read.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Guys help the doctor told me I had a cold but then this man showed up and offered me a ciggarete what do I do oh God oh [EXPLETIVE]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's like Santa, he can be everywhere at once as long as you don't tell your parents

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And… it wouldn't have a relieving ending if it just had to choose whos it going to watch over. It would have the sad realization that it can never do enough, and there are no second chances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit cliche but i like it.

Aaaaaarghh… im sorry, but i have to let it out: what about its "powers"? What if multiple people are separately dying at the same time? It just… duplicates itself?
I KNOW, this is not the point, and i'll just assume that is is capable. Sorry for asking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Object classes indicate how easy something is to contain. It is pretty much impossible to contain SCP-4999 physically, so it's designated keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My question to you is why is it classified as a Keter? You noted down how the S.C.P. explained that SCP-4999 is self-containing in a sense and doesn't really contain details of it being the cause of any dangerous events or direct deaths, so I don't see why this is classified as something that causes harm or chaos.

item.title: Damn
item.content:encoded:
This is really the one SCP I'd be sure to meet if they existed.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While the writing is solid and I like the concept, I was left feeling a but empty by the end of it, a sort of "Is that it?" feeling, like it needed just a little bit more of a punch or was without a satisfying conclusion. I don't know that anything should be added to it but at the same time it didn't really do it for me. Hesitant novote.

item.title: Re: Why is he keter he is self-containing is he not?
item.content:encoded: While a self-containing Keter is a bit of a paradox, 990 works very similarly. From the Foundation's perspective, they cannot reliably be controlled or contained, hence the Letter classification.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alright i am now wondering what kind of research or experiment the foundation did just to be able to make SCP-4999 pick his pizza on SCP-458.

+1

Edit: Aw i just read the footnotes, sad story :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A comforter for the downtrodden.

I really like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a really well done SCP that I enjoyed a lot, and the fact that this was a 4k entry is even more impressive.

Don't get me wrong I loved the SCP, but the ending with him pre-dating human civilization was a bit much in my opinion, but besides that, it's a great SCP
(Don't smoke)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This Is… Wow. Just wow.
I have always been afraid of death (and I don't smoke and hoped that the guy picked up another hobby, like giving gums but nvm) but this thing almost makes me feel "Well fuck It, at least I am going away Happy"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this works perfectly as a -999. It’s not quite the gargantuan beast that 1000’s typically are, which would’ve been a large break in tradition. It sort of feels like the end of a series, the final chapter, the last song on the album, etc. sort of reminds me of the 001 with the flowers. +1, although it doesn’t really matter anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(unless staff step in).

It is fairly rare to see staff intervening on other user's writing, so that really is not much of an issue.

And my 50 cents on the class is that it doesn't really matter all too much. Personally, I believe the fact that this SCP has an urban legend surrounding it as well as it being truly impossible to contain makes this keter. It is difficult to contain and is known (although in a very simplistic way) by the public. but it really depends on what you view a keter SCP to be and changes for every person. I don't see much reason to try forcing everyone to see things the same way.

And as for the anomaly class, it really just depends on what the writer wants. I mean, unless it being called anomaly class rather than object class will ruin your entire experience with the anomaly, I would not waste time getting worked up about it.

item.title: Re: Why is he keter he is self-containing is he not?
item.content:encoded: The fact that the Foundation cannot contain it, nor can they predict where and when it'll manifest, is exactly why it is Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kinda agree with sir pudding.
But, I see your point Commander.

Do you just prefer "anomaly class"?

"If you lock it in a box, leave it alone, and it easily escapes, then it's probably Keter."
I'm guessing this is why you gave it a Keter classification, but I still think it's a bit odd. I would think it would fit better as a Euclid, but the author has the final word (unless staff step in).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check the date

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yo, why did it change? Why is it Spike now? What the hell?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See you later, space cowboy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 from me, you did a great job conveying emotion in such a simple way.

item.title: Why is he keter he is self-containing is he not?
item.content:encoded: Why is he keter he is self-containing is he not? like the only ones who see him are the dying so its self-containing no special containment procedures are needed outside of destroying any evidence

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it okay if I use this as the basis for a joke SCP? I can give you the draft link if you want, I’d feel better if I had your permission.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Object Classes are based mainly off of difficulty to contain, not danger, seeing how this is uncontained, it's Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why is this Keter? All it does is comfort dying people. That's not dangerous at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It depends how he works really.
If he exists in some form when not appearing to the dying then he would presumably continue to do so, he'd just have nothing to do. How this would affect him depends on how his mind works, which is impossible to judge based on the naturally limited evidence.
If he literally comes into existence shortly before the moment of death then ceases to exist until needed again then yes, he'd vanish. Unless, I suppose, he also performs this action for other sapient species/other realities and they weren't affected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still seems like a bit of an overreaction to me but it's a long way from ruining him. I'm cool with the change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder how this fellow would do in the End of Death canon? Would he vanish completely?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow! If I had to say something about this, is it's everything a proper entry should be!

I love the amount of mystery this entry brings. It has the right amount of, "this guy does what?" with, "oh yeah, I get that, that's pretty cool."

I also love how you were able to make a entry that doesn't involve something deadly. I'd like to say it's nearly a cliché now that SCP's need to be deadly, (not dissing those who do use this, it's just nice to have a breath of fresh air) so kudos to you for coming up with a genuinely good idea that doesn't end with someone getting their body blown to bits or something. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-1313
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-4774
http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-2122

there are quite a few other articles that prefer the more accurate term "anomaly class". so, i guess not "literally every other scp", huh.

but really the answer to your question is "because I said so".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heres a better question then: Why does it say "Anomaly class" when LITERALLY EVERY OTHER SCP (except for the obvious estoreic anomalies) say "object class"?

item.title: Re: you can't smoke in here!
item.content:encoded: I know, I love the idea of an SCP that kind of just quietly sits with someone before they die. 10/10 good boy SCP.

item.title: Re: Why is this SCP Classified as 'Keter?'
item.content:encoded:
Where is the quoted text from?

Object Classes currently says:

Keter-class SCPs are anomalies that are exceedingly difficult to contain consistently or reliably, with containment procedures often being extensive and complex. The Foundation often can't contain these SCPs well due to not having a solid understanding of the anomaly, or lacking the technology to properly contain or counter it. A Keter SCP does not mean the SCP is dangerous, just that it is simply very difficult or costly to contain.

This is uncontained and a threat to normalcy, hence Keter.

For another one like this see SCP-396.

item.title: Why is this SCP Classified as 'Keter?'
item.content:encoded:
"The designation "Keter" is assigned to subjects that both (a) display vigorous, active hostility to human life, civilization, and/or spacetime, and (b) are capable of causing significant destruction in the event of a containment breach."

It doesn't seem to be of danger to any of the listed, I may have misread something, but let's discuss. :)

Awesome job by the way, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is heartbreaking.
Also remarkably bittersweet.
+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A common interpretation of the Grim Reaper is that it doesn't actually cause the deaths per-say, rather it moves the people to an after-life.
